input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The **physical twin system** studied in the context is the **serial robot MABI MAX 100**, an industrial robot designed for precision manufacturing tasks. The system comprises **key mechanical components** such as the **motor and gearbox**, which are critical for its kinematic and dynamic behavior. The operational domain is **manufacturing automation**, where the robot performs **high-precision movements** under real-time monitoring. The system is characterized by **complex kinematic chains**, **joint friction dynamics**, and **sensor-instrumented feedback loops** (e.g., encoders, force/torque sensors) to enable accurate state tracking. The physical twin is selected for digital twinning due to its **highly dynamic, multi-DoF (degrees-of-freedom) structure**, **real-time data acquisition requirements**, and **need for predictive maintenance** (e.g., joint friction identification) to enhance operational accuracy and reliability.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin system implements a **Digital Shadow** architecture, defined as a **passive, one-way data flow** between the physical robot (MABI MAX 100) and its digital representation. The primary service provided is **contextual data aggregation and abstraction**, where raw sensor data (e.g., joint angles, torque, friction forces) are collected, processed, and stored for **specific analytical purposes**, such as **identifying joint friction** to improve kinematic modeling accuracy. Unlike active Digital Twin services (e.g., control or optimization), this system **does not push corrected parameters back to the physical system** but instead serves as a **supportive analytical tool** for engineers. Target users include **manufacturing engineers and maintenance teams**, who leverage the aggregated data for **diagnostic insights** and **model refinement** without direct intervention in the robot's operation.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin system is implemented using **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for **structural modeling** of the robot's architecture, including its components (e.g., motor, gearbox), interfaces, and interconnections (as depicted in **Fig. 3**). For **behavioral simulation and dynamic analysis**, **MathWorks MATLAB Simulink** is employed to model the robot's kinematic and dynamic behavior, enabling **real-time or near-real-time simulation** of its movements and friction dynamics. The system integrates **heterogeneous data sources** (e.g., sensors, PLCs) and uses **aggregation and abstraction methods** (e.g., time-series processing, statistical modeling) to derive meaningful insights. No mention of AADL, MontiArc, or general-purpose languages (C++, Java) is made for this specific implementation; these are referenced only as **general examples of tools in the broader Digital Twin/Shadow domain** (Section II.A). The system relies on **model-based systems engineering (MBSE) methodologies** to ensure consistency between the physical and digital representations.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_time_scale: The Digital Twin system described in the provided documents does not explicitly detail synchronization frequencies, time scales for services, latency requirements, or temporal granularity needs. However, the context implies that **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies (e.g., SysML, ADLs like AADL, or MontiArc) are used to model system behavior, which inherently requires **temporal alignment** between physical and digital components. For example, **SysML IBDs (Internal Block Diagrams)** and **state machines in SysML v1.7** or **MontiArc Automaton** may enforce discrete or continuous time modeling, depending on the simulation tools (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink** or other simulation methods [29, 30]) integrated with the digital twin. Latency and granularity would depend on the specific use case, but the documents suggest that **real-time or near-real-time synchronization** is implied for active components (e.g., digital twins acting as interpreters of data flows [33, 34]). No explicit time-scale metrics (e.g., Hz, ms, or sampling intervals) are provided in the text.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The document describes a **single, focused Digital Shadow instance** (e.g., *FrictionShadow* for a robot's first axis) rather than a constellation of multiple Digital Twin instances. The **hierarchical structure** is derived from **MBSE methodologies** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM) that decompose system modeling into **abstraction layers** (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers), but these layers are **not explicitly instantiated as separate Digital Twin instances**. The document does not address **centralized vs. decentralized architectures**, **coordination mechanisms between twins**, or **scope of responsibilities** for individual instances, as the focus remains on a **unified modeling framework** (SysML, ADLs) for a single physical entity's digital representation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The Digital Twin system’s orchestration is defined by **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL, MontiArc**, and **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, which model systems from a **component & connector perspective**, including interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral coordination is achieved through **state machines (SysML v1.7)** and **MontiArc Automaton**, while detailed behavior is integrated via **simulation tools (MathWorks Simulink)** or **general-purpose code (C++, Java)**. The system emphasizes **heterogeneous tool integration** to manage complexity, but does not explicitly detail **resource management** (e.g., priority scheduling, load balancing) or **system-wide coordination mechanisms** beyond the ADL/IBD frameworks. The architecture follows **modular, component-based patterns** with explicit separation of concerns (e.g., functional vs. physical layers).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration: The document mentions **MQTT-based streaming** of sensor time series data as the primary mechanism for external data exchange, but does not specify broader **interoperability standards** (e.g., OPC UA, XML/JSON APIs) or **enterprise system integrations** (e.g., SCADA, MES, ERP). Horizontal integration is implicitly supported by the **MBSE toolchain** (SysML, Simulink, C++/Java), which enables cross-tool communication via **model exchanges** (e.g., SysML profiles) and **simulation interfaces**, though no explicit **ETL pipelines**, **API gateways**, or **mediator patterns** are described. Dependencies on external systems are limited to **sensor data ingestion** (MQTT) and **active processing components** (e.g., software interpreters for digital shadows), with no mention of broader ecosystem integration.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin system incorporates **heterogeneous computational models and data components** structured across multiple abstraction layers, including **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**, as defined by Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE). Key model types include:
- **Geometric models** (implicitly referenced via architectural descriptions, though not explicitly detailed),
- **Behavioral models** (e.g., state machines in **SysML v1.7** or **MontiArc Automaton** for logical architecture),
- **Analytical models** (integrated via simulation tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose languages such as **C++/Java** for detailed behavior).

Data sources are aggregated through **contextual data traces**, including sensor inputs, operational logs, and external datasets, which are abstracted and processed for specific purposes (e.g., performance monitoring, predictive maintenance). The system employs **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **ACME, AADL, MontiArc, or SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** to define **component-connector perspectives**, including interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Model relationships are established via **tool interoperability frameworks** (e.g., linking SysML models to Simulink for behavioral simulation) and **ontology-based integration** (implied for stakeholder alignment). Data management approaches include **aggregation, abstraction, and purpose-specific processing** of traces, often leveraging middleware or ETL pipelines to ensure consistency across heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives.
extracted_characteristics.physical_acting_components: The Digital Twin system incorporates **permanent magnet synchronous motors** as primary actuators, driven by a **closed-loop control system** with defined operational constraints. Each actuator operates within a **maximum working range of [-90°, 90°]** and achieves a **peak speed of 75°/s**. Actuation is governed by **remote control interfaces** (likely embedded in the Digital Twin’s simulation/control layer) to enable real-time adjustments. Safety constraints are implied through the **limited range and speed thresholds**, though explicit safety protocols (e.g., fail-safes, emergency stops) are not detailed. The system’s **addressing mechanisms** for actuators are inferred to be tied to the **component-connector models** in ADLs (e.g., MontiArc or SysML IBDs), ensuring precise mapping between digital and physical entities.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: The Digital Twin system integrates **sensing infrastructure** comprising **two position encoders per drive**, which provide real-time positional feedback for system monitoring and control. Sensors are distributed across the physical system to capture critical operational metrics, with data transmission rates and sampling frequencies aligned to support **robust, real-time identification** of system states. While the document references 'sensors or estimated' data for digital shadow contexts, the explicit mention of **position encoders** (as part of the drive system) confirms their role in the sensing architecture. The spatial distribution of sensors ensures coverage of key operational parameters, though specific accuracy/precision specifications or transmission protocols (e.g., Ethernet, CAN bus) are not detailed in the provided text.
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations: The Digital Twin’s **fidelity and validity** are constrained by its **passive data flow architecture**, where the digital shadow lacks autonomous feedback mechanisms to correct physical system deviations. Validation is implicitly tied to **simulating expected behavior** using integrated models (e.g., SysML, Simulink), but explicit **Verification and Validation (V&V) methodologies** (e.g., formal testing, uncertainty quantification, or error correction protocols) are not described. Uncertainty sources (e.g., sensor noise, model approximations) are not quantified, and **quality assurance processes** rely on the passive aggregation of data traces rather than active calibration. The system’s reliance on **one-way data flow** implies limitations in real-time error detection, though the robustness of behavioral models (e.g., state machines in SysML) may infer indirect validation through consistency checks against simulated outputs.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction:
The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in Digital Twin (DT) systems involves a **unidirectional, automated data flow** from physical assets to their digital representations, characterized by the following technical aspects:
  - **Data Types Transmitted**: Contextual data traces (e.g., sensor readings, operational logs, environmental parameters) aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes, including real-time and historical data.
  - **Communication Protocols**: Not explicitly detailed in the provided documents, but implied to support interoperability with **heterogeneous systems** (e.g., IoT devices, PLCs, or industrial controllers) via standardized protocols like **OPC UA, MQTT, or RESTful APIs** (inferred from MBSE integration requirements).
  - **Transmission Frequencies**: Not specified in the document; however, the context suggests **real-time or near-real-time** data acquisition to maintain synchronization with physical systems, as implied by the need for **active processing components** (e.g., digital shadows require timely data for aggregation).
  - **Event Triggers**: Data collection is triggered by **asynchronous events** (e.g., sensor alerts, state changes) or **periodic polling** (frequency unspecified) to ensure continuous monitoring.
  - **Data Quality Assurance**: While not detailed, the document emphasizes **aggregation and abstraction** of data traces, implying **preprocessing steps** such as filtering, normalization, or anomaly detection to ensure consistency and reliability before integration into the DT model.
  - **Data Integration Mechanisms**: Integration occurs via **model-based systems engineering (MBSE) methodologies** (e.g., **SysML, ADLs like AADL, or MontiArc**) to merge heterogeneous data into a unified digital representation, often combined with **simulation tools** (e.g., Simulink) for behavioral modeling.

  The document distinguishes this from Digital Twins by noting that digital shadows are **passive** and lack **active feedback loops** to the physical system.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The technical network infrastructure for the Digital Twin system’s digital shadow component is explicitly tied to **MQTT** as the communication protocol for streaming sensor time series from physical objects to the digital representation. The document does not provide details on network topology (e.g., star, mesh, or hierarchical), bandwidth requirements, latency specifications, or redundancy mechanisms. Security protocols for network communication are also not mentioned. While the document acknowledges the need for heterogeneous data integration in broader system engineering contexts, it does not elaborate on the underlying network architecture beyond the MQTT protocol for data transmission.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The document does not specify the **deployment infrastructure** for Digital Twin systems. While it mentions the use of **modeling tools** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, C++/Java) and **architecture description languages** (e.g., AADL, SysML), there is no mention of hosting platforms (cloud, edge, or on-premise), **computational/storage requirements**, **scalability mechanisms**, or **deployment models** (e.g., containerization, microservices). The document also does not reference specific technologies like Kubernetes, Docker, or cloud providers (AWS, Azure). The focus is on **modeling and abstraction** rather than deployment infrastructure.
extracted_characteristics.life_cycle_stages: The lifecycle of a Digital Twin (DT) system is structured through **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies, encompassing multiple abstraction layers: **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. The transition between stages is managed via **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**, **ACME, AADL (Architecture Analysis & Design Language), MontiArc**, or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, which define **component-connector perspectives** (e.g., interfaces, interconnections, and composition). Behavior modeling is further refined using **state machines in SysML v1.7** or **MontiArc Automaton**, while detailed simulations are integrated via tools like **MathWorks Simulink**, other simulation engines, or general-purpose programming languages (e.g., **C++ or Java**). Lifecycle governance involves **heterogeneous stakeholder integration** and **multi-tool interoperability**, ensuring alignment across **requirements, functional, logical, and physical models**. Transition management between phases adheres to structured methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM (Object-Oriented Systems Engineering Methodology)**, **SPES (Systematic Process for Engineering Systems)**, or **SMArDT (Smart Digital Twin Methodology)** to maintain consistency and traceability across evolving DT representations.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The **twinning process** and **Digital Twin evolution** are governed by **MBSE methodologies** that emphasize **multi-layered modeling** (requirements → functional → logical → physical) and **heterogeneous tool integration**. **Quality assurance** is implicitly supported through **formalized architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **SysML, AADL, or MontiArc**, which enforce structured component-connector modeling and behavioral specifications (e.g., state machines). **Testing and validation** are facilitated by **simulation tools (Simulink, other simulation engines)** and **general-purpose code (C++, Java)**, ensuring behavioral fidelity. **Version control** and **evolution management** are critical but not explicitly detailed in the document; however, the use of **ADLs and MBSE frameworks** implies **structured versioning** of models (e.g., via **SysML diagrams, MontiArc automata, or simulation models**) to track changes. The **SMArDT methodology** and **RFLP framework** suggest iterative refinement, where **logical and physical layers** evolve based on **requirements and functional updates**, enabling adaptive DT evolution. **Automated data aggregation** (as referenced in digital shadow contexts) may also inform DT updates, though explicit **CI/CD pipelines** or **model-driven engineering (MDE) workflows** are not mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Digital Twin system supports **decision-making** through **analytics and processing capabilities** derived from **model-based simulations** (e.g., **Simulink, other simulation tools**) and **behavioral models** (e.g., **SysML state machines, MontiArc Automaton**). **Insights** are generated via **data aggregation and abstraction** from **contextual traces** (as defined in digital shadow frameworks), enabling **predictive or prescriptive analytics** for stakeholders. **User interaction methods** are not explicitly detailed, but the integration of **heterogeneous tools** suggests **visualization interfaces** (e.g., **SysML diagrams, simulation dashboards**) for decision support. **Confidence measures** for recommendations are implicitly tied to the **fidelity of MBSE models** and **simulation accuracy**, though no specific metrics (e.g., confidence intervals, error thresholds) are provided. The system lacks explicit **AI/ML integration** or **real-time analytics frameworks**, relying instead on **deterministic modeling** and **structured simulation outputs for insights.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations:
The Digital Twin system referenced in the provided documents incorporates **safety-critical design principles** through the **safety cell** concept, which restricts the maximum working range of robotic assets (e.g., the MABI robotic arm) to predefined operational boundaries. This ensures physical safety by limiting motion to non-hazardous zones, as implied in the context of robotic manufacturing applications (Document [44]).

For data transmission, the system employs **MQTT (Message Queuing Telemetry Transport)** protocols (Document [52]) to stream sensor data from physical assets to the Digital Twin, enabling real-time monitoring and control. However, **no explicit mention of encryption (e.g., TLS), authentication mechanisms, or threat mitigation strategies** is provided in the documents. The system’s reliance on MQTT alone implies potential vulnerabilities unless additional security layers (e.g., access control or firewalls) are implemented externally, which are not detailed in the provided sources.

No references to **cybersecurity frameworks (e.g., ISO/IEC 62443)**, **RAMI 4.0 security layers**, or **safety-critical system certifications** (e.g., IEC 61508) are present in the documents. The **Asset Administration Shell (AAS)** (Documents [46], [47], [50]) is described as a standardized data model for digital assets but does not explicitly address security governance or privacy compliance in the provided excerpts.

conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_1_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the system as the 'serial robot MABI MAX 100' and details its components ('motor', 'gearbox'), operational domain ('manufacturing tasks', 'machining'), and specific characteristics like friction dynamics and sensors. The text explicitly mentions the robot model, its structure, and the use of encoders. The reference to 'predictive maintenance' is supported by the text's discussion on monitoring changes due to 'wear' and 'continuous identification' of friction models. Quotes: 'The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX 100', 'first axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor... followed by a cycloidal gear', 'two position encoders per drive', 'continuous... identification of joint friction'.

The description correctly identifies the service as a 'Digital Shadow' characterized by a 'passive, one-way data flow' and 'contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction'. It accurately identifies the specific service purpose as the 'detection of friction loss' to improve accuracy, rather than active control. However, it claims the target users are 'manufacturing engineers and maintenance teams', which is not explicitly stated in the provided text (which mentions 'digital shadow designer' and general 'stakeholders'). Despite this unsupported claim regarding the specific user roles, the technical description of the service is highly specific and correct. Quotes: 'digital shadow is characterized by an automated one-way data flow', 'digital shadow as a set of contextual data traces and their aggregation and abstraction', 'purpose is the understanding of the relations and detection of friction loss'.

The extraction precisely identifies the tools used: 'SysML' for structural modeling (Fig. 3) and 'MathWorks MATLAB Simulink' for behavioral simulation (Fig. 4). It correctly notes that while general tools like 'AADL, MontiArc' are mentioned in the background section (II.A), they are not used for this specific implementation, which relies on the specific tools listed in Section IV. The mention of 'concrete realization' and 'incarnates' concepts is well-grounded in the text. Quotes: 'robot’s structure as a component & connector SysML model', 'executable simulation models of MathWorks MATLAB Simulink', 'concrete realization for our use case'.

ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
